specific_curriculum_outcomes.description: 1.1 listen to music, conversation and environmental sounds for personal enjoyment
1.2 demonstrate interest, curiosity, engagement in sharing the experiences of others and with oral stories and information sharing.
1.3 use social listening and speaking skills to interact with a variety of audiences with sensitivity and respect
1.4 interact and collaborate with the teacher and children who have diverse interests, backgrounds and languages
1.5 become aware of how effective listening enhances understanding
1.6 observe how tone, fluency and intonation impact meaning and mood
1.7 use Home Language(s) and, as Standard English develops, share their thoughts, feelings and questions
1.8 develop increasing clarity and focus when sharing stories or experiences
1.9 engage in active phonological awareness activities and word play to discriminate between various sounds in their environment, letters of the alphabet, rhyme and meaningful sound patterns*
1.10 develop and apply vocabulary and language structures to enhance their understanding of how to communicate ideas with purpose and focus

inclusive_assessment_strategies: The teacher will enter information into the checklist for Listening and Speaking Outcomes 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 1.5, 1.7 and 1.8 based on observation and conversation with the learners.

Language Arts – Observation and Conversation: The teacher will enter information into the checklist for Listening and Speaking Outcomes 1.1, 1.2, 1.3, 1.4, 1.5, 1.6, 1.7 and 1.8 based on observation and conversation with the learners.

inclusive_learning_strategies: Introduction to the Integrated Unit: Language Arts and Science: Discussion & Exploration Stations

The teacher will show learners large pictures of weather symbols (sun, rain, wind, clouds) and ask them to name each one. The teacher will ask the learners to look outside and tell if they see what the pictures show. Then, the teacher will inform the learners that they will be learning about the weather. The teacher will ask the learners the question 'Is the weather important?'
Afterwards, in small groups, the learners will be rotated in four named weather stations; Sun, Rain, Clouds, Wind, upon

Language Arts, Science and Art: Weather Walk
The learners will be taken on a short walk outside of the classroom. They can either go for a walk around the school or to the playground. The learners will be asked to use their different senses; sight, hearing, touch, and smell while on the walk. For example; they will feel the temperature outside or the grass/ plants, see the clouds or dry or wet areas or people and animals outside, listen to sounds like the wind or rain. Then the learners can return inside and discuss all their observations by saying how the weather affects them and others, as well as, what they may need to wear or do differently based on the conditions outside.

The learners will be given canvases or white blank paper along with their water paints and paint brushes, to paint a picture of outside to include people, or animals or plants.

Language Arts, Mathematics, and Science: Picture Discussion
The teacher will give each learner a picture at random illustrating a weather symbol, such as; sun, rain, clouds and wind. The teacher should ask the learners what does the picture make them think of? The teacher will explain to learners that the weather is what we feel and see outside at the time we are looking out, like if it is sunny, rainy/stormy, windy or cloudy. After, the learners will be shown two large pictures of a Sunny Day and a Rainy Day (shown below) which will be posted on the board. Then, focusing on one picture at time, the learners will engage in a THINK-PAIR-SHARE activity, where they would describe the pictures by stating what is happening, what are they wearing, etc. (Please consult the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for explanation of the Think Pair Share strategy).

read the number aloud. The same will be repeated for every group of objects in the picture, such as tress or animals or things. Then as an extension, especially to more advanced learners, the teacher can ask learners to identify which group has the most, or the least or the same amount.
Language Arts, Mathematics, Science, and Music: Video Presentation
Learners will view the following music video on “What’s the weather like today?”
What's The Weather Like Today | Weather Song For Kids

The learners will talk about the things they see in the video, and answer the question asked by the main character at the end of the video: “What’s the weather like today where you live?” Then the teacher will play the video a second time, so the learners can dance along and perform some of the actions as best as they could while the video is being played. At the end of the video, the learners and the teacher will talk about any new words the learners might have heard or learnt in the video; such as puddles, lightening, thunder and stormy.
Shape Weather Craft

The teacher will draw three simple weather scenes on a poster board or whiteboard (sunny day, rainy day or windy day). Space will be left for learners to add shapes which were already pre-cut into circles, triangles squares, rectangles, etc., and provided by the teacher. The teacher will explain to the learners that they will be creating a weather scene using different shapes.
Introduction to Week Three: Language Arts and Science: Outdoor Picnic / Discussion

The teacher will organize an outdoor picnic with the learners. It could be under the shade of a large tree near the school, or in a nearby park. Learners will be asked to act like police detectives and observe their surroundings through sight, touch and smells; such as dry patches of grass and other areas with fresh green grass or plants, animals feeding, muddy areas if any, puddles if any, dry and dusty ground, movement of leaves on trees and movement of the sea or a river if it can be seen from a distance.

The teacher will ask the learners the essential question for the week: How does the weather make a difference to the land and water around us? Learners will explore the area in a safe way while sharing their observations.

Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies (Extension to Outdoor Picnic Activity): Shared Writing. The teacher will have learners gather in a circle and discuss the outdoor picnic. The learners will be asked to share their favourite parts of the picnic and their observations about the weather. The teacher will write down on either chart paper or board, some of the keywords the learners mention about the picnic and the weather. The learners will be encouraged to think about: What the weather was like (sunny, windy, cloudy, etc.), How the weather felt (hot, cold, warm, etc.), Activities they did while outside because of the weather (collected leaves, jumped over puddles of water, played a game, etc.). During that time, the teacher will inform the learners that they will be writing a short story together about their outdoor picnic, by using the keywords from the discussion to complete sentences that are given and read by the teacher. The emphasis on keywords should include the high frequency/sight words for the week (jump, little, help, my) and the emphasis on the sounds (/m/ as in ‘my’, and /h/ as in ‘help’). Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for additional ideas to help children develop their high frequency/sight words, and also ideas for leading a Shared Writing and Shared Reading experiences. Examples of sentence starters that the teacher may put on the chart and ask for suggestions from learners to complete, such as: On Monday, we had a picnic and the weather was ___________. We felt ___________, so we ___________ outside. Then the teacher will read aloud the completed story to the learners, pointing to each word as it is read. Learners will be encouraged to participate in reading aloud (Shared Reading). The teacher will explicitly discuss the Science SCOs: 3.1.1, 3.1.2, 3.2.3, 3.2.4 with the children. After completing the shared writing task, the learners will draw one picture to represent their favourite picnic moment. The learners will be given tape to display their pictures around the completed story.

Language Arts, Science, and Music – Mr. Sun Song: The teacher will play a video with the lyrics on the screen for learners to learn and sing along to the song: ‘Oh, Mr. Sun’ Oh, Mr. Sun | Nursery Rhymes | Classic | Little Fox | Animated Songs for Kids (youtube.com). The teacher should have learners get into positions that they feel comfortable while learning the song. The learners can clap their hands or stamp their feet in time with the rhythm of the song. After performing the song a few times, the teacher and the learners will discuss each line of the song. The learners will say what they understand by each line. The learners will be asked, Do they like the sun? Do the land and rivers/sea like the sun? During that discussion the teacher can help learners understand the positive and negative impact of the sun. This is a song that the children could sing every day when the class fill in the daily weather chart (see Week Two), if the weather is sunny. If the weather is rainy, the class could sing The Itsy Bitsy Spider song.
Science & Language Arts: Plant Observation/ Show and Tell

The teacher would have asked learners before class to each bring in a potted plant. The learners will get turns to show and talk about their plants to the class. They can name their plants, describe their colours, where it is grown/ or found, and how they think their plants grow. After the learners have had their turns, the teacher will ask them what they think is their plant's favourite weather to grow, and also how the weather conditions help their plant to grow.
Then, the learners will each draw their plant and write a sentence with the help of the teacher about what kind of weather conditions help their plant to grow.

Language Arts and Science: Field Trip to a Local Farm- The learners will visit a local farm where the farmer will show and speak with them about how the weather helps his plants (fields) and animals. The farmer will explain how the rain, sun, and wind help make a difference to the plants and animals on his farm. The farmer can also share ways he conserves or uses the rainwater during the dry season, so the learners can understand the importance of the weather. The learners can come prepared to ask questions to the farmer.
Language Arts: Shared Writing (Extension to Field Trip Activity)
Sight Words Emphasis: my, help
Letter Sounds Emphasis: /h/, /m/

The teacher will use a large globe and a large flashlight to help support this lesson. The teacher can begin by asking the learners what is sunlight. Also, why is sunlight important? The teacher will show the learners a globe and explain what it is. Then, the teacher will use the flashlight to simulate sunlight shining on different parts of the globe. This can be done by spinning the globe and holding the flashlight steadily as the different parts are shown on the globe. The teacher will explain to the learners the effects of the sunlight in providing light and warmth to things on the earth which causes changes and makes some things grow. The learners can also take turns trying out the simulation activity with the flashlight and the globe or map. The teacher can also have the learners to pretend to be some of those things on the earth that the sunlight gives light and warmth and have them act it out. For example, the learners can pretend to be a forest and start as small as seeds or small plants and as the flashlight shines on them, they grow tall by standing up and opening their arms like branches on trees. Also, the learners can be taken outside to feel the warmth of the sunlight on different things; such as the soil, pavement, rocks, buildings, and plants/ grass.

The teacher can begin by asking the learners ‘How does the weather affect us?’ Then, the learners will view the video (see the link above) on the effects of the Sun. The teacher will explain to the learners that they will be discussing the video afterward. The following questions can be asked by the teacher: 1. What did you learn about the Sun? 2. How does the weather affect the ocean? 3. What can happen if there is no Sun? Learners’ responses will be recorded by the teacher. This is a Shared Writing experience (please see Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for further information). The teacher will draw a big outline of a sun on a large piece of paper. The children will be invited to draw small sketches of people, places, and things that are effected by the sun. The teacher might want to show the video a second time to remind the children of some things they saw that they could put on the big sun mural.
Introduction to Week Four: Language Arts, Science and Arts/Craft: Create Weather Journals
The learners will be asked the question for the week: How do we know about the weather? Their responses will be recorded by the teacher for future reference throughout the week.

Then the learners will be given sheets of white blank paper, two sheets of hard paper for front and back of a book, some crayons, stickers, glitter, glue, etc. They will be asked to use those things to create their own Weather Watch Journal. The teacher will be helping learners to put their journals together and show them completed examples of some weather journals.

One way to know about the weather is to look outside and observe. The teacher will tell the children that they will act as weather watchers to observe and record daily weather patterns every day for Week Four of the unit. The learners will be asked to look outside each day and observe the weather. Discuss what they see and draw a picture in their weather journals which they created. Then write or circle which word for the weather type (sun, rain, wind, clouds) is most observed under their picture. Also, each page of their drawing must be labelled according to the day of the week being observed.

At the end of the week, there will be a discussion on any weather patterns they notice, and be asked questions by the teacher, such as: - How many sunny / rainy days did we have? - What was the weather like most of the time? - How can the sky tell us about the weather?
The teacher will ask the learners the question, ‘How do we know about the weather?’ The class may look at the chart made in the ILS (above) and take part in a Shared Reading of that chart.
Then, the teacher can have learners view a video (see below) of how scientists or meteorologists tell us about the weather through observations and different tools.
During the video, the teacher may pause and discuss some of the vocabulary that is introduced: meteorologist, radar, satellite, patterns.
After watching the video, the learners will say ways that were shown in the video which tell us about the weather.

The teacher will review with the learners how they can know about the weather. The teacher will ask the learners if they have ever heard or seen someone on the radio or television talk about the weather. The teacher and the learners will discuss this by mentioning the different things the person may say about the weather. Then, the teacher will have learners observe a video on weather forecasts explaining simply how it is done and why it is important (see link below)
https://youtu.be/_0S8VpU3bTY?si=KUQPxk3S3Z5ryCqt
After the video, the learners will say what they understand about the video.

Then, the children will listen to a simple weather report (see link below). The children will be told that they will choose a partner and create their own weather report through role-play with the help of the teacher.
https://youtu.be/_O3tiKkcP3s?feature=shared
The learners can have fun doing their versions of weather reporting. They can “perform” their weather reports to the rest of the class, who will act as the audience.

The teacher will review with the children what a weather report is and why it is important. For example; weather reports help us know what the weather will be like so we can dress appropriately and plan our activities well. If it rains for the day, then we may not be able to go outside for a Physical Education Class.
The teacher will display on a large chart, or on the board, a simple weather report.
This chart will be used for Shared Reading, with the teacher emphasizing the high frequency/sight words and letter sounds, for this unit, and from the Belongings Unit. (Please
Introduction to Week Five: Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies: Read Aloud

Before: The teacher will begin the week by placing a sign on the wall or the board with the motto: Be prepared; not scared. The teacher will read the motto and explain to the learners that it means being ready for anything that might happen, so that we won’t have to be afraid. The teacher will discuss with learners some situations where it is important to be prepared such as; a storm or hurricane, no power/electricity, a fire, etc. Then, the teacher will inform learners that they will be listening to a story which tells about being prepared for bad weather.
Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions of how to enhance the children’s development of Language Arts skills through a Read Aloud/Viewing experience.

During: The learners will listen to the story being read aloud – Clifford And the Big Storm https://youtu.be/7qeL4LWWT4Y?feature=shared. As the story is being read to the learners, the teacher will pause occasionally to discuss how the characters in the story are getting prepared and staying safe for the storm.

After: When the story is done, the learners will talk about their favourite parts of the story. The teacher may feel it is necessary to point out that in a real storm, no one would be able to go back to their houses and try to do the things that Clifford did! The children will draw a picture, and write a sentence to fit the idea of “Be prepared; not scared”.
Language Arts, Social Studies, and Science: Guest Speaker

The teacher will bring in a Fisherman from the community to discuss with the learners about his work, and how important the weather is to him. The fisherman will also talk about how he prepares for the weather and how the motto- Be prepared; Not Scared applies to him. The fisherman will talk to learners about listening to the advice of the weather report and not being able to go out to sea in some weather conditions, The fisherman will also say how he protects his boat and other belongings from severe weather.

Before the speaker comes to the classroom, the teacher will tell the learners who is coming and what they will talk about. The teacher will encourage the learners to think of some questions they might want to ask this visitor to the classroom.
After the speaker has chatted with the learners, the learners would use that opportunity to ask questions.
After the guest speaker leaves, the learners would complete the sample recording sheet for a guest speaker as provided at the beginning of this unit, with the help of the teacher if needed.
The learner might also help the teacher write a Thank You note for the guest speaker.

Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies: Teacher Show and Tell. The teacher will bring an Emergency Preparedness Kit to show learners. The kit will include; a flashlight, extra batteries, a first aid kit, water bottles, a small portable radio, extra cash, some non-perishable food items, a waterproof bag to place valuables or important documents, and a change of clothes. Emergency Kit Prediction Activity: Instead of showing the Emergency Kit immediately, start by placing it in a mystery bag or box and asking learners to predict what might be inside. To spark ideas, you might ask: “What items would we need if we couldn’t get to a store or had to stay safe at home?” Encourage learners to think of different situations where they might need specific items (e.g., if the lights went out, if it was cold, etc.). Show the learners the items in the kit, one at a time. The learners will name each item from the emergency kit and say how those items help in an emergency.

Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies: Creating a Stormy Night, Safety Drills and Sirens. The teacher will help the children to re-create the experience of a stormy night. Some learners will volunteer to play musical instruments; such as drums or containers, shak-shaks, cymbals or metal lids, wind chimes, and rain sticks to create a stormy night. The learners will identify the various sounds that are represented in a storm from their instruments, while the other learners will explain how they will prepare themselves when such sounds are heard. Then learners will switch roles and do the same. The learners can also describe the sounds as loud or soft, and fast or slow. The learners will name their favourite sound and their most disliked sound, if any. The teacher will ask the learners if they know what drills are. The learners will be given opportunities to practice some safety response drills by following the instructions of the teacher. The teacher will explain that

The teacher will ask the learners the question for the week: How do people protect themselves and their belongings from the weather? The learners who may have already memorized the motto by now will be asked to say it aloud: Be Prepared, Not Scared. The teacher will explain to the learners that they will be viewing a short video on severe weather preparedness (see the link below).

From the video, the learners will identify all the preparedness tips they observed. The learners will be asked to give other ways people can protect themselves, their animals, or their belongings which were not included in the video.

The teacher can help them come up with those tips. For example, trim branches on trees or cut them down, place pets inside, or let them loose if possible. Repair broken fences, if you live close to a river or sea, you may have to move away until the storm passes, and secure vehicles in garages or somewhere away from trees or electric poles.
After, the teacher will ask the learners to draw one way they can help their family prepare for a storm or hurricane. The learners will show and talk about their drawings to the class.

The teacher will ask the question for the week: How do people protect themselves and their belongings from the weather? The teacher will hold a discussion with the children about what might happen if there is too

The teacher dresses up in a weather appropriate attire, e.g. for rainy/stormy weather: a raincoat, hat, water boots and an umbrella for Circle Time and discussion. Then, the teacher will ask the learners the following questions to evoke thinking about the weather; e.g. What am I wearing? Have you or anyone else you know dresses like this? Do we always dress like this? When are we not dressed like this? What other ways do you dress when going outside/outdoors?

After learners share their responses, the teacher will explain that the weather affects many parts of our lives, just like the clothes we wear. Learners will be asked, ‘Is the weather important?’ Why is the weather important? The teacher will make notes of learners’ free responses. After, the learners will select materials from a large table placed in the front of the classroom in order to create one item that can be used outdoors for a weather condition, then show their craft work with the class.

The teacher will ask learners to tell various activities which they do for various weather conditions. Afterwards, the learners will be told that they will be playing a game, and for this game they will be placed in small groups and be given one sheet of blank paper and a pencil for drawing. Learners will be told to choose someone in the group to draw on behalf of the group, and they can change that person if they wish after each round. The game will require learners to draw an item in response to each round of questions asked by the teacher based on things we do or wear because of the weather: Round 1: If it is raining, what can we use to stay dry? Round 2: When it is sunny, what do we wear to protect our eyes? Round 3: What is big enough to hide the sun in the sky? Round 4: What can be eaten and melts quickly in your hands, on a sunny day Round 5: What is something children like to do outside on a windy day?

A Sports variation of the game can be done by learners being placed in teams and participate in a relay or tag race (indoor/ outdoor) to find the items placed at a distance in front of them for each round of questions posed by the teacher. The teacher has the choice to change the questions listed above to coincide with the activity or items presented. Learners will be encouraged to cheer for each of their team members.

The teacher will ask the learners to recite the days of the week and to state which day of the week is today. The learners will be asked questions such as, “What is the weather like today?” and “How did they prepare or come dressed for the weather at school today?”

The learners will be shown two drawn symbols on the board /chart which represents the sun and the rain. The learners will be asked to identify each symbol and look outside to say which of the two can be seen. Afterwards, the teacher will use a coloured chalk/marker and shade inside of the one which the learners have identified that can be seen. If both can be seen at the same time, then the teacher informs learners that both can be shaded in. Learners are given their own worksheets which contain the same drawings to do the same, along with one other row (see the example below). Learners will be required to colour the symbol next to either Day 1 or Day 2 of the week they are observing.
Introduction to Week Two: Language Arts and Social Studies: Read Aloud
The teacher will engage learners in a Read Aloud for the week. The story can be read in parts throughout the week focusing on various lessons, until it is completed.
Read Aloud Story “What Will the Weather Be Like Today?” https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BgvbpxTX0rM

Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for ideas of ways to enhance the development of Language Arts outcomes through a Read Aloud. Also, provided here are some suggestions for steps for a Read Aloud.

Before: The teacher will ask learners the weekly question: “What different kinds of weather do we have?”, and invite them to share their favourite types of weather. The teacher will also ask “What seasons do we have?”
The teacher will begin the Read Aloud video by showing the image of the book cover to be read and ask the learners what they think the story is about based on the title and the illustrations.

The teacher will have learners review the key vocabulary words related to weather, such as; rainy, sunny, windy and cloudy by completing a matching activity of the words to a picture which best describes it. The words can be read to learners if needed.
During: The teacher will begin the Read Aloud video. As pages are read, the teacher will pause occasionally to
Language Arts, Mathematics, Science, and Social Studies: Class Weekly Weather Chart

The teacher will create a simple weather chart together with the learners to hang up in the classroom, and to work with each day of the week. This chart should be repeated for Weeks 3, 4, and 5 of this unit. To make the chart, learners can be given various responsibilities to draw, colour, and select pictures on a computer or print with the teacher, images representing the different types of weather. Or paste, design, help cut and hang the chart up in the classroom.
Along with the chart, there can also be a sentence strip where learners have to add the missing word to describe the weather. “Today it is .......”

Each day a different learner will be designated to be the ‘weather reporter’ and place the appropriate weather symbol on the chart based on what is observed outside, as well as find the appropriate word to complete the sentence which describes the weather.
https://www.pinterest.com/pin/practical-life--683280574698657521/ (weather chart with pictures).

Through a whole class discussion with the teacher, the learners will interpret the class weather chart at the end of every week by responding to the following questions: 1. Which type of weather did we have the most this week? 2. Which type of weather did we have the least this week?
-Analyse and Discuss a Video. All About the Weather: https://youtu.be/ZfamR09Jklw

- A Nature Walk- comparison with the video: Learners will talk about the weather outside by going on a nature walk. They will talk about what they see, hear and feel. They will count clouds, water puddles, etc. They can state the colour of the clouds, sun, water puddles, etc. Learners will state what other weather conditions they know that were not displayed in the video or on their nature walk (stormy, snowy, etc.) The learners will be asked to draw the type of weather being experienced presently.
Weather “I Spy” Game

This game can be used to help the learners observe and identify the different types of weather conditions. The teacher will explain to the learners that the weather changes from day to day, and briefly review the different weather conditions such as, rainy, sunny, cloudy and windy, while using a weather chart with the various weather symbols. Then the teacher will take the learners either to a large window or outside and tell them that they will be playing a game; “I Spy”. The teacher can explain the rules of the game to the learners by going first. The teacher will state “I spy with my little eye something fluffy and white (or any other weather condition). Learners will take turns guessing. After a correct guess, a learner can try out the game. After several rounds of the game, the learners will discuss the different types of weather conditions identified and asked to share their observations.

Language Arts, Social Studies and Science: Seasonal Changes
The teacher will use floor tape to divide the class into half (two groups) to represent our two seasons: wet season and dry season. Give one half of the class cut-outs sunglasses and the other half of the class mini cut-outs umbrellas. The learners will be asked why they think the classroom is set up in this way. Then, the teacher will also ask the learners if they have ever noticed that sometimes it rains a lot, while other times it does not rain much at all. The teacher will explain the result of two seasons with different types of weather.
The teacher should introduce the idea of “weather pattern” and explain that there is a pattern of sunny days, or a pattern of rainy days, depending on the season. The weather pattern repeats every year, with the season. One season is wet, which the umbrella group represents, and the other season is dry, which the sunglasses group represents.
Language Arts, Science and Social Studies: Shared Reading
The teacher will begin by asking the learners whether they enjoy it when it rains, and what are their favourite things to do when it rains. Then the teacher will play the song ‘Rain, Rain Go Away’
Rain, rain, go away,
Come again another day,
Little children want to play.
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Zu6o23Pu0Do

The teacher can put the words to the poem on a chart. Then read the poem aloud to the learners making sure to use an engaging tone and expressive gestures. After, for shared reading the teacher can support the learners in doing the following:
Choral Reading – Invite the learners to read the poem together with the teacher, while pointing to each word as it is read.
Language Arts and Social Studies,: Expression through the Arts

Weather Wheel Craft: Learners will use large paper plates and materials of their choice to make a weather wheel showing four types of weather (sunny, rainy, cloudy, and windy). Part of their weather wheel will include an arrow to point to the type of weather needed. Learners will use their weather wheels to track the changes in the weather throughout the day and to help predict the weather. Their observations will be shared to the whole class, where they would need to state the different kinds of weather.
Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies: Song/Rhyme - ‘The Itsy-Bitsy Spider’ -

The teacher will present the rhyme with pictures on a large chart. The teacher will read the title of the rhyme and ask learners if they have ever heard or sang the rhyme before. The teacher will sing the rhyme and have learners to do the same. The teacher can ask the learners to discuss what the rhyme is about line by line.
Language Arts and Science: Circle Time with Pictures and Drawing Activity

The learners will gather in a circle and the teacher can begin by asking the learners the following questions: - What will happen if the weather report says there will be no rain for a few days? - What will happen if it rains heavily for a long time during the day?

Then the learners will be shown large pictures or videos of severe weather conditions; namely, a storm/bad weather. The learners will be asked to name the type of weather and talk about the things they notice in the picture. The learners will be asked if they have ever experienced a storm/ bad weather and what was it like. The teacher will ask the learners how they found out about the storm or the bad weather. Also, the teacher will ask the learners if it was important to know about the storm or bad weather before it happened.
The learners will be asked to draw and colour a picture of a stormy day. The children might also be encouraged to print a sentence under their drawing, telling about their picture.

2.3 connect background knowledge to the titles and pictures of fiction and nonfiction passages to build a foundation of understanding

2.7 demonstrate understanding of some environmental print and pictorial information
2.8 begin to apply comprehension strategies to visualize, predict and connect
2.9 demonstrate understanding by responding to read-alouds with images, model making, discussions, or temporary writing.
2.10 connect words and images in pre-emergent and emergent level texts to background knowledge
2.11 Recognize and use a variety of high frequency words of personal importance, such as names and pre-emergent level high-frequency words
2.12 participate in shared reading and use the meaning and flow of the language to anticipate upcoming words
2.13 begin to demonstrate fluency and phrasing during shared reading, independent and guided reading of emergent level passages
2.14 identify an increasing number of letter names and letter sounds, beginning with those of personal importance
2.15 use known letter sounds to decode upcoming words in emergent level passages

inclusive_assessment_strategies: The teacher will enter information into the checklist for Reading and Viewing Outcomes 2.11 and 2.14 based on observation and conversation with the learners.
Social Studies- Observation and Conversation
The teacher will enter information into the checklist for Myself – Spatial Thinking Outcome 3.7 based on observation and conversation with the learners.
inclusive_learning_strategies:
a signal from the teacher. At each station, the learners will explore through observation and hands-on, some weather associated props and materials. For example; in the sunny station, may have sunglasses, visors and sun hats. The rainy station may have umbrellas, rain boots, raincoats and a simulated puddle. The cloudy station may have a cloudy sky collage with white or gray cotton balls or small pieces of soft fabric which learners can help complete by gluing on more cloud formations. The windy station may have learners play a game among their group members where they would have to use their mouths to blow some light weight items, such as strips of paper or leaves across a table, and the child who blows it the farthest wins.

After the station activities, the teacher will gather all the learners and they will be asked, ‘Which was their favourite weather station?’ Why was that their favourite station? Is that also their favourite kind of weather?
Sunny day- Use yellow circles for the sun and blue rectangles for a clear sky
Rainy day- Use blue circles for raindrops and grey rectangles for clouds
Windy day- Use green rectangles and triangles for trees and plants

Ask the learners to take turns adding a shape and talk about how each shape is shown in the weather scene. After, the learners will review the different shapes. The teacher may ask questions like; “How many triangles did we use for the plants?” and “What shape did we use for the sun?”

Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for additional ideas to help children develop their high frequency/sight words, and also ideas for leading a Shared Writing and Shared Reading experiences. Examples of sentence starters that the teacher may put on the chart and ask for suggestions from learners to complete, such as: On Monday, we had a picnic and the weather was ___________. We felt ___________, so we ___________ outside. Then the teacher will read aloud the completed story to the learners, pointing to each word as it is read. Learners will be encouraged to participate in reading aloud (Shared Reading). The teacher will explicitly discuss the Science SCOs: 3.1.1, 3.1.2, 3.2.3, 3.2.4 with the children. After completing the shared writing task, the learners will draw one picture to represent their favourite picnic moment. The learners will be given tape to display their pictures around the completed story.

The teacher will explain to the learners that they will be making a thank you card together. The learners and the teacher will discuss the importance of saying thank you, and the teacher will inform them that they need to thank the farmer for the field trip. The teacher will help the children to remember what they saw and did on the trip to the farm. The teacher will ask learners what they would like to say on the card and write down those ideas on a chart or board. The teacher will choose the most popular ideas and begin writing on the chart or board. The teacher should find a way to incorporate the sight words: my, help; into the writing.
For example: Mr. Joe, Thank you for your help at the farm. It was a good day. My friends and I had fun.

As each sentence is written the learners will help the teacher find the words; my & help. After the words are pointed out, the learners will read the sentences together with the teacher. The teacher will also ask learners to identify where they hear the sounds; /h/ and /m/ in the words being read. The learners will practice saying those sounds with the help of the teacher and give other examples of words where they hear those sounds. Some of those words will be written on a separate side of the chart or board by the teacher.
Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for ideas to help children develop their recognition

The teacher can begin by asking the learners ‘How does the weather affect us?’ Then, the learners will view the video (see the link above) on the effects of the Sun. The teacher will explain to the learners that they will be discussing the video afterward. The following questions can be asked by the teacher: 1. What did you learn about the Sun? 2. How does the weather affect the ocean? 3. What can happen if there is no Sun? Learners’ responses will be recorded by the teacher. This is a Shared Writing experience (please see Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for further information). The teacher will draw a big outline of a sun on a large piece of paper. The children will be invited to draw small sketches of people, places, and things that are effected by the sun. The teacher might want to show the video a second time to remind the children of some things they saw that they could put on the big sun mural.
Introduction to Week Four: Language Arts, Science and Arts/Craft: Create Weather Journals
The learners will be asked the question for the week: How do we know about the weather? Their responses will be recorded by the teacher for future reference throughout the week.

Then the learners will be given sheets of white blank paper, two sheets of hard paper for front and back of a book, some crayons, stickers, glitter, glue, etc. They will be asked to use those things to create their own Weather Watch Journal. The teacher will be helping learners to put their journals together and show them completed examples of some weather journals.

One way to know about the weather is to look outside and observe. The teacher will tell the children that they will act as weather watchers to observe and record daily weather patterns every day for Week Four of the unit. The learners will be asked to look outside each day and observe the weather. Discuss what they see and draw a picture in their weather journals which they created. Then write or circle which word for the weather type (sun, rain, wind, clouds) is most observed under their picture. Also, each page of their drawing must be labelled according to the day of the week being observed.

At the end of the week, there will be a discussion on any weather patterns they notice, and be asked questions by the teacher, such as: - How many sunny / rainy days did we have? - What was the weather like most of the time? - How can the sky tell us about the weather?
The teacher will add those ways to the class chart (Shared Writing)

Please consult the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for specific ideas of ways to enhance the development of Language Arts SCOs through a Shared Writing or Shared Reading experience.

The teacher will review with the children what a weather report is and why it is important. For example; weather reports help us know what the weather will be like so we can dress appropriately and plan our activities well. If it rains for the day, then we may not be able to go outside for a Physical Education Class.
The teacher will display on a large chart, or on the board, a simple weather report.

The teacher will ask the learners if they know what severe weather means. The teacher will explain that severe weather is a dangerous weather condition. Such as the conditions in a storm or hurricane, which can cause loss of life and damage to property.
The teacher will play a video (see link below). https://www.youtube.com/live/2CZU82ImIEc?si=fEyiRBSPJEslGcj

After viewing the video for a few seconds, the learners will be asked to talk about what they observed. Also, the learners will discuss if they have ever seen or heard of this type of severe weather, and how they think they can prepare for it.
The teacher may ask the children the question for the week: How do people protect themselves and their belongings from the weather?
In particular, the teacher may ask how children and their families protect their belongings from heavy rain.

The teacher should make a list of ways to protect belongings from heavy rain. This would be a Shared Writing experience. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions to enhance Language Arts skills during this experience.

Then, the teacher will ask the learners what other items they can include in the kit. For example, shoes, medicine, and eyeglasses. Reflection Discussion: “Why do we need these items?” After revealing and discussing each item, ask learners open-ended questions like, “How could this help us in an emergency?” or “What would happen if we didn’t have this item?” This will encourage them to think critically about each item’s purpose and importance. Afterward, the learners will draw their emergency preparedness kit, and the teacher will help them label their drawings.
Echo Reading – The teacher will read one line at a time and have the learners read each line.
(Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for more specific ideas of ways to help children develop Language Arts skills through Shared Reading).
Then, the teacher will engage learners in some questions about the poem: For example; “Why do the children want the rain to go away?” and “Will reciting the poem actually make the rain go away?”
Introduction to Week Two: Language Arts and Social Studies: Read Aloud
The teacher will engage learners in a Read Aloud for the week. The story can be read in parts throughout the week focusing on various lessons, until it is completed.
Read Aloud Story “What Will the Weather Be Like Today?” https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=BgvbpxTX0rM

Before: The teacher will ask learners the weekly question: “What different kinds of weather do we have?”, and invite them to share their favourite types of weather. The teacher will also ask “What seasons do we have?”
The teacher will begin the Read Aloud video by showing the image of the book cover to be read and ask the learners what they think the story is about based on the title and the illustrations.

The teacher will have learners review the key vocabulary words related to weather, such as; rainy, sunny, windy and cloudy by completing a matching activity of the words to a picture which best describes it. The words can be read to learners if needed.
During: The teacher will begin the Read Aloud video. As pages are read, the teacher will pause occasionally to

point out to illustrations or ask comprehension questions or make predictions which would help guide those following main concepts being covered in the story: ‘What is Weather?, Seasons, What is the Sun?, What is Wind?, What are Clouds? What is a Thunderstorm? Rainbows, and ‘What is your favourite Type of Weather?’

The teacher can use engagement activities with learners to interact with the story parts read. When reading about sunny weather, learners can be invited to stand up and do their best sunshine dance. When reading about the rainy weather, learners can mimic sounds of the rain using various parts of their bodies or actions. When reading about the windy weather, learners can pretend to blow things away or act like the items being blown away.
After finishing the story, the teacher can ask learners to name the different types of weather from the story and express their feelings about the different types of weather.

Learners can talk about their favourite parts of the story and share what they learned. Or, learners can draw their favourite part of the story using their own illustrations and present it to a friend.
Learners will go outside and describe the type of weather based on information they learned from the story read.
Language Arts, Mathematics, Science, and Social Studies: Class Weekly Weather Chart

The teacher will create a simple weather chart together with the learners to hang up in the classroom, and to work with each day of the week. This chart should be repeated for Weeks 3, 4, and 5 of this unit. To make the chart, learners can be given various responsibilities to draw, colour, and select pictures on a computer or print with the teacher, images representing the different types of weather. Or paste, design, help cut and hang the chart up in the classroom.
Along with the chart, there can also be a sentence strip where learners have to add the missing word to describe the weather. “Today it is .......”

Each day a different learner will be designated to be the ‘weather reporter’ and place the appropriate weather symbol on the chart based on what is observed outside, as well as find the appropriate word to complete the sentence which describes the weather.
https://www.pinterest.com/pin/practical-life--683280574698657521/ (weather chart with pictures).

Through a whole class discussion with the teacher, the learners will interpret the class weather chart at the end of every week by responding to the following questions: 1. Which type of weather did we have the most this week? 2. Which type of weather did we have the least this week?

Language Arts, Social Studies, and Science: Guest Speaker

The teacher will bring in a Fisherman from the community to discuss with the learners about his work, and how important the weather is to him. The fisherman will also talk about how he prepares for the weather and how the motto- Be prepared; Not Scared applies to him. The fisherman will talk to learners about listening to the advice of the weather report and not being able to go out to sea in some weather conditions, The fisherman will also say how he protects his boat and other belongings from severe weather.

Before the speaker comes to the classroom, the teacher will tell the learners who is coming and what they will talk about. The teacher will encourage the learners to think of some questions they might want to ask this visitor to the classroom.
After the speaker has chatted with the learners, the learners would use that opportunity to ask questions.
After the guest speaker leaves, the learners would complete the sample recording sheet for a guest speaker as provided at the beginning of this unit, with the help of the teacher if needed.
The learner might also help the teacher write a Thank You note for the guest speaker.

Then, the teacher will ask the learners what other items they can include in the kit. For example, shoes, medicine, and eyeglasses. Reflection Discussion: “Why do we need these items?” After revealing and discussing each item, ask learners open-ended questions like, “How could this help us in an emergency?” or “What would happen if we didn’t have this item?” This will encourage them to think critically about each item’s purpose and importance. Afterward, the learners will draw their emergency preparedness kit, and the teacher will help them label their drawings.
After, the teacher will ask the learners to draw one way they can help their family prepare for a storm or hurricane. The learners will show and talk about their drawings to the class.

The teacher will ask the question for the week: How do people protect themselves and their belongings from the weather? The teacher will hold a discussion with the children about what might happen if there is too
much sunlight and heat on their bodies, or on some of their belongings.
The teacher will explain that the children will be building a model of a shelter that is used to keep people, animals, and things safe from the sunlight.
The learners can choose the type of shelter they will build and the reason for building it. Examples of shelters are houses, kennels, tents, schools, and churches.

The learners will select materials from the teacher’s Arts/Crafts table to create their shelters. The teacher is encouraged to let the children start to build their shelters, then stop the class. Ask all the children to step away from their work and tell about any problems they are having or how they have figured out how to solve problems (e.g. to get the walls of their shelter to stand up).
After this discussion, all the children may return to their building with some new ideas of how to solve any problems they might be having.
When all the shelters are completed, the learners will label them with the help of the teacher and place them on display for other learners and teachers, as well as parents, to view.
The teacher will be observing for children’s Concepts of Print. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for a list of Concepts of Print.

The letters/sounds c (k), e, h, r, m, d will be the focus. The teacher might do some “stand-alone” lessons to introduce the children to these letters/sounds. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for many ideas for teaching children letters/sounds.

Phonemic Awareness skills will commence and the focal point will be on initial phoneme isolation in words related to this Integrated Unit, such as “rain”. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for many ideas for engaging learners in phonemic awareness skills.

The high frequency (sight) words it, not, run, up, we, away, find, help, here, jump, little, my will be the focus. The teacher might do some “stand-alone lessons” to introduce these words. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for many ideas for teaching high frequency words.
The teacher is encouraged to find opportunities to emphasize those letters/sounds/phonemes/high frequency words with the children throughout the ILS for the unit.

The teacher will ask the learners to recite the days of the week and to state which day of the week is today. The learners will be asked questions such as, “What is the weather like today?” and “How did they prepare or come dressed for the weather at school today?”

The learners will be shown two drawn symbols on the board /chart which represents the sun and the rain. The learners will be asked to identify each symbol and look outside to say which of the two can be seen. Afterwards, the teacher will use a coloured chalk/marker and shade inside of the one which the learners have identified that can be seen. If both can be seen at the same time, then the teacher informs learners that both can be shaded in. Learners are given their own worksheets which contain the same drawings to do the same, along with one other row (see the example below). Learners will be required to colour the symbol next to either Day 1 or Day 2 of the week they are observing.
Language Arts, Mathematics, Science, Social Studies and Arts/ Craft: Weather Art
Weather Sorting Game - Mathematics

Using picture cards or small objects to represent various weather conditions (e.g. rain, sun, wind, clouds), the learners will work together in small groups to categorize the picture cards or objects based on a weather condition. The children will count how many cards or small objects are in each group (rain, sun, wind, clouds). The teacher will go around the various groups observing and asking questions to learners, while stating how the weather is important and it affects our daily lives.

The teacher will discuss the form (below) with the learners. Learners will be given time to do their drawings. Then, as a whole class, the teacher will ask the learners to compare the activities they have drawn in each section to check if they do similar things or different things based on the weather outside.
Draw a picture of something you do when there is lots of......
End of Week One: Slideshow Presentation Science, Social Studies and Language Arts
The focus of this ILS is to return to the question of the week: Is the weather important to me and my family?

The teacher will use pictures on a slideshow to reinforce the importance of weather to learners, from prior class discussions on the topic. Since the depictions on the slideshow would have been familiar to learners, the teacher would ask the learners to talk about them, and discuss if this kind of weather is important to them and their family.

After, the learners will be asked to create a drawing of a weather scene. For example, a rainy day at home, a sunny day at the beach or a windy day at the park. Learners will use materials such as crayons, paints and coloured paper.

Learners will write a sentence about their drawing, explaining how that weather is important for them and their family. For those who have difficulty writing, the teacher will either provide the learner with a model or act as a scribe for the learner. Learners will share their finished work with the whole class.

The children might enjoy this read-aloud (which has some unexpected elements) as a final prompt to consider how the weather makes a difference to people. Farmer Joe’s Hot Day. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9rwZ-7H9Ok0
3. How many days in the week were rainy/ sunny/ windy?
4. Did we have any days with more than one type of weather?

Patterns over time: The teacher will examine the chart with the learners at the end of each week to look for patterns. Perhaps the learners will note that there were three sunny days in a row. Perhaps they can look at the 5 days of the week and notice that there was a pattern of sunny days, with no rain, or something like that. Then, they will compare this to other weeks during the unit.
The teacher will use this class weather chart to review the days of the week with the class.
Throughout this ILS, the teacher will make direct connection to Science outcomes 3.3.2, 3.3.3, and 3.3.5.
Concluding Event – End of Week Five, and End of the Integrated Unit Language Arts, Science, Social Studies, Mathematics

The teacher will review the essential question for the unit by asking the learners: Is the Weather Important? The children will be reminded of the answers they gave at the beginning of the unit, and asked to consider if they have more ideas now.

The teacher will take the children on a Gallery Walk to remind them of all the experiences they had during the 5 weeks. In this way, the teacher will prepare the children to be “tourist guides” for their family members, or other adults from the school, or children from another class.

Family members, or other adults from the school, or children from another class will be invited to come for a Celebration of Learning. Each child will take a few visitors around the room and explain what they learned.
The session might end with a cake or another special treat to celebrate “Being Prepared; Not Scared”.

a signal from the teacher. At each station, the learners will explore through observation and hands-on, some weather associated props and materials. For example; in the sunny station, may have sunglasses, visors and sun hats. The rainy station may have umbrellas, rain boots, raincoats and a simulated puddle. The cloudy station may have a cloudy sky collage with white or gray cotton balls or small pieces of soft fabric which learners can help complete by gluing on more cloud formations. The windy station may have learners play a game among their group members where they would have to use their mouths to blow some light weight items, such as strips of paper or leaves across a table, and the child who blows it the farthest wins.

After the station activities, the teacher will gather all the learners and they will be asked, ‘Which was their favourite weather station?’ Why was that their favourite station? Is that also their favourite kind of weather?

Exit Ticket: The learners will be asked to draw to show one way the weather is important. The learners will be helped to write a sentence about their picture and they will be encouraged to explain their picture to the teacher. To further enhance the children’s learning, the learners might share their ideas verbally with a partner.

Then, the teacher will engage learners in a follow-up discussion of why the weather is important by asking them to think and share some other things they do with their families on a sunny day and on a rainy day.

The picture with more votes will remain posted on the board, while the other will be taken down. The teacher will ask learners to help identify all the people in the picture, and as they do, a small sticky note will be placed on each person in the picture. The learners will be told that they will be counting the number of people in the picture to determine the quantity. They will be informed that they will start from the one, point to each sticker and read aloud to ensure they have all the objects in the group.
