The teacher will begin by asking learners the question for the week: How does the weather make a difference to the land and water around us? Then, the teacher will explain to the learners that they will be in groups to discuss this and act out how their type of weather can affect the land and water around them. The teacher will divide the class into four groups and give them costumes or props representing four different weather conditions (sun, rain, clouds, wind)
NB: Each group will represent and play out a different weather condition:
Group 1: Rain
Group 2: Sun
Group 3: Wind
Group 4: Clouds

After the learners are placed in their groups, the teacher with the help of learners through probing questions, will briefly explain what each weather condition does: Rain – Falls from the clouds, waters the plants, helps the plants to grow, creates water puddles on the ground, and fills the rivers and seas. Sun – Shines in the sky, makes the ground feel hot/ warm, helps the plants to grow, and dries up water on the ground. Wind – Blows across the land, rivers, and the sea, it can move the clouds and branches or leaves on trees and can make waves on the sea. Clouds – Can block the sun to prevent it from shining brightly, can make the ground feel cool, and can bring rain. Learners will act out each scenario one group at a time. Then there will be a discussion by the teacher and the learners about what happened to the land and water in each scenario. Questions such as the following can be asked by the teacher: - What did the sun do to the land/water puddles? - How did the rain help the plants? - What happened to the land and rivers when it rained? - How did the wind affect the sea? Then, the teacher will ask the learners the essential question: Is the weather important?

The teacher can begin by asking the learners ‘How does the weather affect us?’ Then, the learners will view the video (see the link above) on the effects of the Sun. The teacher will explain to the learners that they will be discussing the video afterward. The following questions can be asked by the teacher: 1. What did you learn about the Sun? 2. How does the weather affect the ocean? 3. What can happen if there is no Sun? Learners’ responses will be recorded by the teacher. This is a Shared Writing experience (please see Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for further information). The teacher will draw a big outline of a sun on a large piece of paper. The children will be invited to draw small sketches of people, places, and things that are effected by the sun. The teacher might want to show the video a second time to remind the children of some things they saw that they could put on the big sun mural.

End of Week Three: Language Arts & Science Group Project - Creating a Shoebox Diorama Note: This can be done in the classroom with groups as explained below, or done by the learners at home. The teacher will ask the learners discuss the question for Week Three: How does the weather make a difference to the land and water around us? In particular, the teacher will ask the children to state examples of how the sun can make a difference to the land

The teacher will explain to the learners that there are tools that people can use to know about the weather. The teacher will use the example of wind since it cannot be seen like the sun or rain, but it can be felt or observed by the movement of trees and waves on the water, etc. Some weather forecasters have fancy instruments to know about the wind. Children, too, can use something to know about the wind – a paper windmill!
The teacher will show the video (below) to the children and explain that this video is giving us the directions for how to make a paper windmill.

The teacher will work with a small group of children at a time, to help them make their own windmill. (The other children might be working on another craft, or playing games. Or, one or two additional adults might be invited into the class to help everyone make a paper windmill).

Afterward, learners will go outside and test their windmills to find out if there is wind, and by the slow or quick movement of the sections, they can determine the degree of the wind. The learners can use their tools to predict the weather, as will be told by the teacher.

The teacher will review with the learners how they can know about the weather. The teacher will ask the learners if they have ever heard or seen someone on the radio or television talk about the weather. The teacher and the learners will discuss this by mentioning the different things the person may say about the weather. Then, the teacher will have learners observe a video on weather forecasts explaining simply how it is done and why it is important (see link below)
https://youtu.be/_0S8VpU3bTY?si=KUQPxk3S3Z5ryCqt
After the video, the learners will say what they understand about the video.

Then, the children will listen to a simple weather report (see link below). The children will be told that they will choose a partner and create their own weather report through role-play with the help of the teacher.
https://youtu.be/_O3tiKkcP3s?feature=shared
The learners can have fun doing their versions of weather reporting. They can “perform” their weather reports to the rest of the class, who will act as the audience.

The teacher will review with the children what a weather report is and why it is important. For example; weather reports help us know what the weather will be like so we can dress appropriately and plan our activities well. If it rains for the day, then we may not be able to go outside for a Physical Education Class.
The teacher will display on a large chart, or on the board, a simple weather report.
This chart will be used for Shared Reading, with the teacher emphasizing the high frequency/sight words and letter sounds, for this unit, and from the Belongings Unit. (Please
Language Arts, Social Studies, and Science: Picture Scenarios and Video

The learners will be shown various pictures of people outside in the weather. The teacher will ask the learners to say how the people in the pictures are protecting themselves from the weather. Also, the teacher will ask the learners to say besides what they are wearing, what can they do so they will not get hurt. For example; walking carefully, staying off wet rocks, or staying inside if you don’t have to go out when it rains. Drinking lots of water and staying in the shade as much as possible when it is sunny and hot outside. Covering their faces when it is windy/ dusty outside. The teacher will note learners’ responses.

The teacher will ask the learners if they know what severe weather means. The teacher will explain that severe weather is a dangerous weather condition. Such as the conditions in a storm or hurricane, which can cause loss of life and damage to property.
The teacher will play a video (see link below). https://www.youtube.com/live/2CZU82ImIEc?si=fEyiRBSPJEslGcj

After viewing the video for a few seconds, the learners will be asked to talk about what they observed. Also, the learners will discuss if they have ever seen or heard of this type of severe weather, and how they think they can prepare for it.
The teacher may ask the children the question for the week: How do people protect themselves and their belongings from the weather?
In particular, the teacher may ask how children and their families protect their belongings from heavy rain.

The teacher should make a list of ways to protect belongings from heavy rain. This would be a Shared Writing experience. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions to enhance Language Arts skills during this experience.

Then, the teacher will ask the learners what other items they can include in the kit. For example, shoes, medicine, and eyeglasses. Reflection Discussion: “Why do we need these items?” After revealing and discussing each item, ask learners open-ended questions like, “How could this help us in an emergency?” or “What would happen if we didn’t have this item?” This will encourage them to think critically about each item’s purpose and importance. Afterward, the learners will draw their emergency preparedness kit, and the teacher will help them label their drawings.

Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies: Creating a Stormy Night, Safety Drills and Sirens. The teacher will help the children to re-create the experience of a stormy night. Some learners will volunteer to play musical instruments; such as drums or containers, shak-shaks, cymbals or metal lids, wind chimes, and rain sticks to create a stormy night. The learners will identify the various sounds that are represented in a storm from their instruments, while the other learners will explain how they will prepare themselves when such sounds are heard. Then learners will switch roles and do the same. The learners can also describe the sounds as loud or soft, and fast or slow. The learners will name their favourite sound and their most disliked sound, if any. The teacher will ask the learners if they know what drills are. The learners will be given opportunities to practice some safety response drills by following the instructions of the teacher. The teacher will explain that
there are different drills for different emergencies, such as those caused by the effects of severe weather.

One drill can be done by a family at home, when the parents/caregivers help the children to learn about places at home that they can use as a safe place if there is a storm or hurricane. When families and children and people at a school practice what to do in an emergency, they are following the motto: Be Prepared; Not Scared.

The teacher will also discuss the use of sirens as a warning mechanism due to severe weather conditions, The teacher will show the learners a picture of a siren and explain how it is used to alert people that it is time to evacuate or leave an area because there is a dangerous weather situation approaching. The teacher will play the audio of a siren for the learners to listen to. After, the teacher will ask the learners if drills and sirens are important.

From the video, the learners will identify all the preparedness tips they observed. The learners will be asked to give other ways people can protect themselves, their animals, or their belongings which were not included in the video.

The teacher can help them come up with those tips. For example, trim branches on trees or cut them down, place pets inside, or let them loose if possible. Repair broken fences, if you live close to a river or sea, you may have to move away until the storm passes, and secure vehicles in garages or somewhere away from trees or electric poles.
After, the teacher will ask the learners to draw one way they can help their family prepare for a storm or hurricane. The learners will show and talk about their drawings to the class.

The teacher will ask the question for the week: How do people protect themselves and their belongings from the weather? The teacher will hold a discussion with the children about what might happen if there is too

The teacher dresses up in a weather appropriate attire, e.g. for rainy/stormy weather: a raincoat, hat, water boots and an umbrella for Circle Time and discussion. Then, the teacher will ask the learners the following questions to evoke thinking about the weather; e.g. What am I wearing? Have you or anyone else you know dresses like this? Do we always dress like this? When are we not dressed like this? What other ways do you dress when going outside/outdoors?

After learners share their responses, the teacher will explain that the weather affects many parts of our lives, just like the clothes we wear. Learners will be asked, ‘Is the weather important?’ Why is the weather important? The teacher will make notes of learners’ free responses. After, the learners will select materials from a large table placed in the front of the classroom in order to create one item that can be used outdoors for a weather condition, then show their craft work with the class.

The teacher will ask learners to tell various activities which they do for various weather conditions. Afterwards, the learners will be told that they will be playing a game, and for this game they will be placed in small groups and be given one sheet of blank paper and a pencil for drawing. Learners will be told to choose someone in the group to draw on behalf of the group, and they can change that person if they wish after each round. The game will require learners to draw an item in response to each round of questions asked by the teacher based on things we do or wear because of the weather: Round 1: If it is raining, what can we use to stay dry? Round 2: When it is sunny, what do we wear to protect our eyes? Round 3: What is big enough to hide the sun in the sky? Round 4: What can be eaten and melts quickly in your hands, on a sunny day Round 5: What is something children like to do outside on a windy day?

A Sports variation of the game can be done by learners being placed in teams and participate in a relay or tag race (indoor/ outdoor) to find the items placed at a distance in front of them for each round of questions posed by the teacher. The teacher has the choice to change the questions listed above to coincide with the activity or items presented. Learners will be encouraged to cheer for each of their team members.
Science and Craft: Extension to Lesson on Poem – Rain, Rain, Go Away

The teacher will explain to learners that in some places in the world, there are people who do not have rain for a very long time, therefore, to get rain, they would make rain sticks, and believed that the sounds that the rain sticks make will call for the rain and cause it to fall. The teacher would ask the learners why do they think those people needed the rain. Then, the teacher will tell the learners that they will be making rain sticks.
Note: Instructions for making a rain stick found here
Cover one end of the paper towel roll with duct tape.
Insert a long rolled-up piece of aluminium foil into the paper towel roll.
Fill the inside of the paper towel roll 1/4 full with the mixture of corn kernels, rice, and small noodles.
Cover the other end of the paper towel roll with duct tape.
DIY Rainstick | Crafts for Kids | PBS KIDS for Parents

The teacher will ask learners to name the different types of weather that they know. They will discuss briefly what each type of weather looks like. The teacher will explain to learners that they will be learning about patterns using pictures of different types of weather. The teacher will explain that a pattern is something that repeats in a predictable way.

Before attempting to use the weather types pictures, the teacher will use the learners to show patterns such as; boy-girl, boy-girl, boy-..... and ask them who comes next: boy or girl. Then another example on a white board/ blackboard to illustrate patterns with two colour shades like; red-yellow, red-yellow, red-...... (Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for additional ways to help children learn about Patterning).
Afterwards, learners will attempt together patterns using the weather type pictures before being asked to do it independently.
Using weather cards learners will complete a simple pattern
Note: For differentiated activity; learners can create weather symbols with playdoh and formulate patterns under the guidance of the teacher.
Introduction to Week Three: Language Arts and Science: Outdoor Picnic / Discussion

The teacher will organize an outdoor picnic with the learners. It could be under the shade of a large tree near the school, or in a nearby park. Learners will be asked to act like police detectives and observe their surroundings through sight, touch and smells; such as dry patches of grass and other areas with fresh green grass or plants, animals feeding, muddy areas if any, puddles if any, dry and dusty ground, movement of leaves on trees and movement of the sea or a river if it can be seen from a distance.

The teacher will ask the learners the essential question for the week: How does the weather make a difference to the land and water around us? Learners will explore the area in a safe way while sharing their observations.

The teacher will draw a line halfway down the whiteboard or blackboard, and label one side wet season and the other side dry season using drawn examples of the same symbols as an umbrella and sunglasses to help distinguish the labels for the learners. Learners will be asked:
• What do you think happens during the wet season?
• How is the dry season different to the wet season?
• What types of activities do people do during each season?
Learners’ responses will be recorded by the teacher in the appropriate column on the board.
The teacher might want to show the video about Wet and Dry Seasons to the class (see below in Additional Resources).
The teacher will tell the children the time (month) that the wet and dry seasons usually begin in their region.

Investigative Matching Activity
Learners will investigate pictures and match them to one of the two seasons in the Caribbean - Wet Season and Dry Season.
The teacher will place learners in small groups and give them some pictures showing plant fields, farms with animals, people, activities and water bodies. In groups the learners will investigate the pictures for clues/ evidence to determine the type of season. A learner from each group will share on behalf of its members by explaining the clues/ evidence found.
Learners will discuss and draw the weather of the day and state their likes and dislikes about that particular type of weather condition.

Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies (Extension to Outdoor Picnic Activity): Shared Writing. The teacher will have learners gather in a circle and discuss the outdoor picnic. The learners will be asked to share their favourite parts of the picnic and their observations about the weather. The teacher will write down on either chart paper or board, some of the keywords the learners mention about the picnic and the weather. The learners will be encouraged to think about: What the weather was like (sunny, windy, cloudy, etc.), How the weather felt (hot, cold, warm, etc.), Activities they did while outside because of the weather (collected leaves, jumped over puddles of water, played a game, etc.). During that time, the teacher will inform the learners that they will be writing a short story together about their outdoor picnic, by using the keywords from the discussion to complete sentences that are given and read by the teacher. The emphasis on keywords should include the high frequency/sight words for the week (jump, little, help, my) and the emphasis on the sounds (/m/ as in ‘my’, and /h/ as in ‘help’). Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for additional ideas to help children develop their high frequency/sight words, and also ideas for leading a Shared Writing and Shared Reading experiences. Examples of sentence starters that the teacher may put on the chart and ask for suggestions from learners to complete, such as: On Monday, we had a picnic and the weather was ___________. We felt ___________, so we ___________ outside. Then the teacher will read aloud the completed story to the learners, pointing to each word as it is read. Learners will be encouraged to participate in reading aloud (Shared Reading). The teacher will explicitly discuss the Science SCOs: 3.1.1, 3.1.2, 3.2.3, 3.2.4 with the children. After completing the shared writing task, the learners will draw one picture to represent their favourite picnic moment. The learners will be given tape to display their pictures around the completed story.

Language Arts, Mathematics, and Social Studies - Weather Likes and Dislikes. The teacher will ask the children some questions and use their answers to complete a data collection chart. The teacher can begin with the category of favourite type of weather (sunny, rainy, cloudy, windy) to collect data for representation in a graph. For example: Favourite Weather table with columns 'Learner Name' and 'Favourite Weather' showing Danny - Rainy, Philip - Sunny, Ruby - Sunny, Sam - Windy. (The teacher may print the first names of the learners in the class in the left column of the chart. Learners will read or identify their names to answer when their name comes up.)
The teacher can have the learners sing the rhyme a few times while following it on a large chart.
Pointing to the pictures in the rhyme, the teacher can engage learners in a discussion of how the land and water are affected by the types of weather we have.

The teacher can ask the learners - ‘what happened in the spout as it rained? The learners may use words like wet, soaked, soggy, slippery; or even flood. The teacher will guide learners as much as possible with questions to get them thinking. Then, the teacher can ask learners if they have ever seen the land get filled with water when it rains and what happens around them when it does.

Language Arts, Science and Social Studies: A Nature Walk – The teacher will explain to learners that they will be taking a weather walk around the school to explore the land and to carry along paper and pencils for drawing. (The teacher might have the papers clipped to a piece of cardboard so it’s easier for the children to handle). The learners will be asked to describe the type of weather outside. The teacher will explain that they will be using their senses; touch, smell, sight, and hearing to look out for ways that the land and water around them are changed by the weather. The teacher can start by giving an example so the learners will understand what they are expected to do. The learners can record their observations through drawing.

Language Arts, Science and Social Studies: Weather Experiments - The teacher will set up stations outside the classroom where simple experiments on the effects of the weather will be performed and discussed by the learners. For example: Station 1: What changes happens to a rock when it rains, and then what changes happens to the rock after it is left a while in the sun? (NB: Sprinkle water and observe what happens)
Station 2: What changes happen to the soil when it rains, and what changes happen to a puddle when it is sunny? (NB: Sprinkle lots of water and observe what happens)
Station 3: What changes happen to water in a large bowl if it is windy? (NB: Use a powerful fan, or hair-dryer for demonstration and observe what happens)
Station 4: What changes happen to a mountain when it rains heavily and for a long time? (NB: Learners will pour cups of water down a model clay mountain and observe what happens)

After the station experiments, the learners will state which station was their favourite and why. They will also say if those were the changes they expected to happen or if before today they have seen those changes happen. Then, the learners can give an idea of a station experiment to observe changes that can happen because of the weather.

NOTE: Regarding Science SCOs 3.3.5 and 3.3.6, (starting in Week Two of this Integrated Unit. the children will be working as a class to collect the descriptive data (sun, wind, etc.) and the numerical data (3 sunny days, 5 windy days, etc.). The children will collect data about the weather, individually, here in Week 4.

Ask the learners to take turns adding a shape and talk about how each shape is shown in the weather scene. After, the learners will review the different shapes. The teacher may ask questions like; “How many triangles did we use for the plants?” and “What shape did we use for the sun?”

The teacher will ask the learners to recite the days of the week and to state which day of the week is today. The learners will be asked questions such as, “What is the weather like today?” and “How did they prepare or come dressed for the weather at school today?”

The learners will be shown two drawn symbols on the board /chart which represents the sun and the rain. The learners will be asked to identify each symbol and look outside to say which of the two can be seen. Afterwards, the teacher will use a coloured chalk/marker and shade inside of the one which the learners have identified that can be seen. If both can be seen at the same time, then the teacher informs learners that both can be shaded in. Learners are given their own worksheets which contain the same drawings to do the same, along with one other row (see the example below). Learners will be required to colour the symbol next to either Day 1 or Day 2 of the week they are observing.
The teacher will use this class weather chart to review the days of the week with the class.
Throughout this ILS, the teacher will make direct connection to Science outcomes 3.3.2, 3.3.3, and 3.3.5.

The teacher can use engagement activities with learners to interact with the story parts read. When reading about sunny weather, learners can be invited to stand up and do their best sunshine dance. When reading about the rainy weather, learners can mimic sounds of the rain using various parts of their bodies or actions. When reading about the windy weather, learners can pretend to blow things away or act like the items being blown away.
After finishing the story, the teacher can ask learners to name the different types of weather from the story and express their feelings about the different types of weather.

Learners can talk about their favourite parts of the story and share what they learned. Or, learners can draw their favourite part of the story using their own illustrations and present it to a friend.
Learners will go outside and describe the type of weather based on information they learned from the story read.

After several rounds of the game, the learners will discuss the different types of weather conditions identified and asked to share their observations.

The teacher should extend this activity by having learners do some “first hand” science experiments on the ground or in spaces outside. Learners can choose a particular place outside, pretend that it is raining by using a water bottle, and then talk about what changes happened to the ground or the space. Then, later during the day, the learners can return outside to where they made puddles on the ground to investigate the area to find out if it has dried up, and what may have caused the puddles to dry, and what they think happened to the water on the ground; Or, if the puddles are still there and why. The learners will return to the class and further try to explain the ideas of the water puddles outside. The learners can act like scientists and draw what happened to the water puddles. The teacher can point out that scientists observe carefully and they also record what they observe, often with words and pictures.
The learners can discuss their observations/ drawings in class in a follow-up lesson.

After the learners are placed in their groups, the teacher with the help of learners through probing questions, will briefly explain what each weather condition does: Rain – Falls from the clouds, waters the plants, helps the plants to grow, creates water puddles on the ground, and fills the rivers and seas. Sun – Shines in the sky, makes the ground feel hot/ warm, helps the plants to grow, and dries up water on the ground. Wind – Blows across the land, rivers, and the sea, it can move the clouds and branches or leaves on trees and can make waves on the sea. Clouds – Can block the sun to prevent it from shining brightly, can make the ground feel cool, and can bring rain. Learners will act out each scenario one group at a time. Then there will be a discussion by the teacher and the learners about what happened to the land and water in each scenario. Questions such as the following can be asked by the teacher: - What did the sun do to the land/water puddles? - How did the rain help the plants? - What happened to the land and rivers when it rained? - How did the wind affect the sea? Then, the teacher will ask the learners the essential question: Is the weather important?
Language Arts, Science, Social Studies and Mathematics: Outdoor Circle Time

The learners will be taken outside to sit under some shade, where they can properly observe the weather. The teacher can begin a discussion by asking the learners, how they or their family know how to dress to go outside. The teacher will ask the learners to do the following to help with the discussion: - Look at the sky (not directly at the sun). Can the sky tell you about the weather? What do you see? - Look at the trees, the rocks, and the land. Can those things tell you about the weather? What do you see? - Feel the grass and how does your body feel? Does the grass feel wet or dry? Do you feel cold, hot, or warm? Can the way the grass feels or the way you feel tell you about the weather?

Afterward, the learners will return inside and talk again about how they or their family know how to dress to go outside. The teacher may need to prompt for answers, if the children only say that they can look outside, or go outside to see what the weather is. The teacher may prompt about what a weather forecast is, and if the children know about a weather forecast being on the radio, or on the television, or on a website. Afterward, learners will go outside and test their windmills to find out if there is wind, and by the slow or quick movement of the sections, they can determine the degree of the wind. The learners can use their tools to predict the weather, as will be told by the teacher.

The teacher will review with the learners how they can know about the weather. The teacher will ask the learners if they have ever heard or seen someone on the radio or television talk about the weather. The teacher and the learners will discuss this by mentioning the different things the person may say about the weather. Then, the teacher will have learners observe a video on weather forecasts explaining simply how it is done and why it is important (see link below)
https://youtu.be/_0S8VpU3bTY?si=KUQPxk3S3Z5ryCqt
After the video, the learners will say what they understand about the video.

Then, the children will listen to a simple weather report (see link below). The children will be told that they will choose a partner and create their own weather report through role-play with the help of the teacher.
https://youtu.be/_O3tiKkcP3s?feature=shared
The learners can have fun doing their versions of weather reporting. They can “perform” their weather reports to the rest of the class, who will act as the audience.

The teacher will review with the children what a weather report is and why it is important. For example; weather reports help us know what the weather will be like so we can dress appropriately and plan our activities well. If it rains for the day, then we may not be able to go outside for a Physical Education Class.
The teacher will display on a large chart, or on the board, a simple weather report.

The teacher will ask the learners if they know what severe weather means. The teacher will explain that severe weather is a dangerous weather condition. Such as the conditions in a storm or hurricane, which can cause loss of life and damage to property.
The teacher will play a video (see link below). https://www.youtube.com/live/2CZU82ImIEc?si=fEyiRBSPJEslGcj

After viewing the video for a few seconds, the learners will be asked to talk about what they observed. Also, the learners will discuss if they have ever seen or heard of this type of severe weather, and how they think they can prepare for it.
The teacher may ask the children the question for the week: How do people protect themselves and their belongings from the weather?
In particular, the teacher may ask how children and their families protect their belongings from heavy rain.

The teacher should make a list of ways to protect belongings from heavy rain. This would be a Shared Writing experience. Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for suggestions to enhance Language Arts skills during this experience.

Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies: Teacher Show and Tell. The teacher will bring an Emergency Preparedness Kit to show learners. The kit will include; a flashlight, extra batteries, a first aid kit, water bottles, a small portable radio, extra cash, some non-perishable food items, a waterproof bag to place valuables or important documents, and a change of clothes. Emergency Kit Prediction Activity: Instead of showing the Emergency Kit immediately, start by placing it in a mystery bag or box and asking learners to predict what might be inside. To spark ideas, you might ask: “What items would we need if we couldn’t get to a store or had to stay safe at home?” Encourage learners to think of different situations where they might need specific items (e.g., if the lights went out, if it was cold, etc.). Show the learners the items in the kit, one at a time. The learners will name each item from the emergency kit and say how those items help in an emergency.

Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies: Creating a Stormy Night, Safety Drills and Sirens. The teacher will help the children to re-create the experience of a stormy night. Some learners will volunteer to play musical instruments; such as drums or containers, shak-shaks, cymbals or metal lids, wind chimes, and rain sticks to create a stormy night. The learners will identify the various sounds that are represented in a storm from their instruments, while the other learners will explain how they will prepare themselves when such sounds are heard. Then learners will switch roles and do the same. The learners can also describe the sounds as loud or soft, and fast or slow. The learners will name their favourite sound and their most disliked sound, if any. The teacher will ask the learners if they know what drills are. Social Studies and Music: Weather Dance
The teacher will play instrumental music and ask learners to dance freely around the room, expressing different types of weather through movement. Learners can move like they’re walking in the rain; stomping their feet in puddles of water as they dance, or swirl their bodies like the wind, or behave like sunbeams. The teacher will provide learners with props like umbrellas, sun hats and scarves to enhance the movements and add fun. Then, learners can select their own instruments to play along, such as tambourines to mimic rain or tapping drums to mimic thunder. Afterwards, learners will be invited to share the sounds that they made or heard and state the type of weather associated with those sounds.

Social Studies and Arts
The teacher will explain to learners that today they will be creating paintings inspired by different types of weather. To engage a discussion, the teacher will ask learners the following questions:
1. What does sunny weather look like?
2. How can we represent windy weather in our paintings?
3. What colours might we use for a rainy day?
Learners will be encouraged to use their imagination to come up with ideas for their paintings. Using large sheets of paper and paints, learners will choose a type of weather and start their artwork. When the paintings are completed, learners will write their names on their art pieces and hang them on the wall in the classroom. The learners will put up an art exhibition, where the teacher will invite another class to view the learners’ work, and the learners would get an opportunity to describe their art.

Language Arts, Science, and Social Studies (Extension to Outdoor Picnic Activity): Shared Writing. The teacher will have learners gather in a circle and discuss the outdoor picnic. The learners will be asked to share their favourite parts of the picnic and their observations about the weather. The teacher will write down on either chart paper or board, some of the keywords the learners mention about the picnic and the weather. The learners will be encouraged to think about: What the weather was like (sunny, windy, cloudy, etc.), How the weather felt (hot, cold, warm, etc.), Activities they did while outside because of the weather (collected leaves, jumped over puddles of water, played a game, etc.). During that time, the teacher will inform the learners that they will be writing a short story together about their outdoor picnic, by using the keywords from the discussion to complete sentences that are given and read by the teacher. The emphasis on keywords should include the high frequency/sight words for the week (jump, little, help, my) and the emphasis on the sounds (/m/ as in ‘my’, and /h/ as in ‘help’). Please see the Curriculum Outcomes and Guide for Effective Strategies for additional ideas to help children develop their high frequency/sight words, and also ideas for leading a Shared Writing and Shared Reading experiences. Examples of sentence starters that the teacher may put on the chart and ask for suggestions from learners to complete, such as: On Monday, we had a picnic and the weather was ___________. We felt ___________, so we ___________ outside. Then the teacher will read aloud the completed story to the learners, pointing to each word as it is read. Learners will be encouraged to participate in reading aloud (Shared Reading). The teacher will explicitly discuss the Science SCOs: 3.1.1, 3.1.2, 3.2.3, 3.2.4 with the children. After completing the shared writing task, the learners will draw one picture to represent their favourite picnic moment. The learners will be given tape to display their pictures around the completed story.

Language Arts, Mathematics, and Social Studies - Weather Likes and Dislikes. The teacher will ask the children some questions and use their answers to complete a data collection chart. The teacher can begin with the category of favourite type of weather (sunny, rainy, cloudy, windy) to collect data for representation in a graph. For example: Favourite Weather table with columns 'Learner Name' and 'Favourite Weather' showing Danny - Rainy, Philip - Sunny, Ruby - Sunny, Sam - Windy. (The teacher may print the first names of the learners in the class in the left column of the chart. Learners will read or identify their names to answer when their name comes up.)

When families and children and people at a school practice what to do in an emergency, they are following the motto: Be Prepared; Not Scared.

The teacher will also discuss the use of sirens as a warning mechanism due to severe weather conditions, The teacher will show the learners a picture of a siren and explain how it is used to alert people that it is time to evacuate or leave an area because there is a dangerous weather situation approaching. The teacher will play the audio of a siren for the learners to listen to. After, the teacher will ask the learners if drills and sirens are important.
